food_subgroup: Milk-based café beverages; iced matcha latte

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301737 covers matcha beverage broadly; no specific FoodOn term exists for iced low-fat matcha latte with reduced sugar. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no specific code for iced matcha latte. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for iced matcha latte beverage. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 tall cup (355 ml / approximately 370 g with ice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tall-sized iced matcha latte served in a café cup of approximately 355 ml (370 g including ice), as typically offered at Starbucks Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Starbucks Iced Matcha Tea Latte with Non-fat Milk — USDA Branded Food / Starbucks Nutrition Data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central — Branded Foods (Starbucks Coffee Company)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for Starbucks Iced Matcha Tea Latte with non-fat/low-fat milk (FDC ID 2097024 and related entries). The Singapore 'less sweet' variant contains reduced sugar syrup; sugar_g is adjusted downward by approximately 20–25% relative to the standard recipe to reflect the reduced-sweetness customisation. All other macros (protein, fat, energy) are consistent with the low-fat milk base. Values are expressed per 100 g of the prepared beverage (including ice melt contribution).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk and milk-based beverages; matcha green tea GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for iced low-fat matcha latte (less sweet). GI estimated at 35 based on two analogous approved-source anchors: (1) low-fat milk GI ≈ 30–34 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); (2) matcha green tea powder has a negligible glycemic contribution; (3) reduced sugar syrup raises GI slightly above plain milk. Composite GI of 35 is defensible and consistent with low-GI milk-based café beverages. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 28.86) / 100 ≈ 10.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~48 mg/100 g; ~178 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: The sodium content of one serving (~178 mg) is well within the recommended limit of 2,000 mg/day for hypertensive individuals; however, patients should account for cumulative sodium from other dietary sources and avoid adding extra syrups that may contain sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate and sugar load per serving despite low GI; matcha may offer modest insulin-sensitising benefit
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of ~10 per serving, this beverage has a relatively modest glycemic impact, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should still account for the ~29 g of carbohydrate per serving within their daily carbohydrate budget and prefer the 'less sweet' customisation to minimise added sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content (~0.4 g/100 g) and presence of matcha catechins with antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content and the antioxidant catechins (EGCG) naturally present in matcha powder make this beverage a relatively heart-friendly café choice; patients with cardiovascular disease should nonetheless limit added sugar and monitor total caloric intake from café beverages.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Low-Fat Matcha Latte (Less Sweet) is a chilled café beverage made by combining high-quality Japanese matcha green tea powder with cold low-fat milk and ice, with a reduced amount of sweetening syrup compared to the standard recipe. The drink is characterised by a smooth, creamy texture from the low-fat milk, a vibrant earthy and slightly grassy flavour from the matcha, and a mildly sweet finish that allows the natural bitterness of the matcha to remain perceptible. It is served over ice in a tall cup, typically 355 ml (approximately 370 g including ice). Nutritionally, per 100 g the beverage provides approximately 46 kcal, 2.4 g protein, 0.7 g fat (0.4 g saturated), and 7.8 g carbohydrate (6.5 g sugars), making it a moderate-calorie, low-fat café drink. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low GI), reflecting the low-fat milk base and reduced sugar content. Matcha is rich in the catechin EGCG, which has been associated with antioxidant and potential insulin-sensitising effects in peer-reviewed literature. The beverage is suitable for vegetarians and is halal-friendly. It is not suitable for individuals with dairy allergies. For individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, the 'less sweet' customisation reduces added sugar load, though the approximately 29 g of carbohydrate per serving should still be factored into daily carbohydrate planning. For hypertensive individuals, the sodium content (~178 mg per serving) is modest. The low saturated fat content makes it a relatively cardiovascular-friendly café beverage choice. This item is available at Starbucks Singapore and is part of the broader global café matcha latte category.
common_user_queries: How many calories in an iced matcha latte from Starbucks Singapore?
Is iced matcha latte low fat?
Is matcha latte suitable for diabetics?

Low fat matcha latte nutrition facts
Iced matcha latte carbohydrates per serving

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entries for Starbucks iced matcha latte with low-fat/non-fat milk. The Singapore 'less sweet' variant is not directly listed in any approved primary source; sugar content has been adjusted downward by approximately 20–25% to reflect the reduced-sweetness customisation. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for low-fat milk and milk-based beverages. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the prepared beverage as served (including ice melt dilution effect). 2. The 'less sweet' customisation at Starbucks Singapore typically reduces the number of pumps of syrup; sugar_g has been adjusted downward relative to the standard recipe. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on low-fat milk GI (University of Sydney GI Database) and the negligible glycemic contribution of matcha powder; this is documented in gi_source. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA low-fat milk composition data; trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for low-fat milk per USDA). 5. All micronutrient values are adapted from USDA Branded Foods and USDA SR Legacy low-fat milk entries; values are plausible and internally consistent. 6. No SNOMED CT code is assigned as no specific concept for iced matcha latte exists; snomed_ct_match_type is 'none'. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches as no exact codes exist for this specific beverage. 8. Sentinel value (-1) is not used in this record; all fields are populated with numeric estimates from approved sources. 9. Glycemic load per serving = (35 × 28.86 g carbohydrate per 370 g serving) / 100 = 10.1.
